content.text:
Here are the results for your query:

## "Emergence of Linear Truth Encodings in Language Models"
The paper is authored by **Shauli Ravfogel** (NYU), **Gilad Yehudai** (NYU), **Tal Linzen** (NYU), **Joan Bruna** (NYU), and **Alberto Bietti** (Flatiron Institute).
Recent probing studies reveal that large language models exhibit linear subspaces that separate true from false statements, yet the mechanism behind their emergence was unclear.
The authors introduce a transparent, one-layer transformer toy model that reproduces such truth subspaces end-to-end and exposes one concrete route by which they can arise.

They study a simple setting in which truth encoding can emerge: a data distribution where factual statements co-occur with other factual statements (and vice-versa), encouraging the model to learn this distinction in order to lower the language modeling loss on future tokens.

In the toy setting, they observe a **two-phase learning dynamic**: networks first memorize individual factual associations in a few steps, then—over a longer horizon—learn to linearly separate true from false statements.

The authors hypothesize that a linear truth code takes advantage of the memorized factual associations: it emerges as a result of the model contrasting the internal prediction it built with the observed attribute.

The findings offer tangible applications for AI safety, particularly regarding hallucinations. If engineers can identify the specific "direction" of truth within a model's internal geometry, they can potentially monitor when a model's generation drifts into the "false" region.
